**#32633** 9:43 PM 🔵 **INPUT test initially passes without full rebuild trigger**

Initial reproduction attempt of issue #5 shows the INPUT functionality working correctly. The program successfully read three lines from input.dat, printed each line in uppercase (HELLO, WORLD, GOODBYE), then printed "End of input" when EOF was reached. The program halted cleanly after 42,988 instructions. This contradicts the expected broken behavior described in the issue (garbage integers, infinite loop, no EOF handling). The issue reporter noted the bug was masked by stale build cache and only appears after forcing a full rebuild by touching all source files to invalidate the cache. The current test may be using cached build artifacts that still work correctly.

Read: ~314, Work: 🔍 1,591

**src/sno_lex.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32634** " 🔵 **INPUT regression cannot be reproduced after full rebuild**

After forcing a complete rebuild by touching all PL/SW source files, the INPUT regression described in issue #5 still cannot be reproduced. The rebuild invalidated all build cache and recompiled all modules, producing the same binary size and behavior as before. The INPUT example continues to work correctly - reading three lines, printing them in uppercase, then printing "End of input" and halting cleanly. Issue #5 was filed earlier on April 11th claiming this exact procedure (touch source files, rebuild, run input example) would trigger garbage integer output and infinite looping. The bug may have been fixed as a side effect of the sno_exec.plsw refactoring completed for issue #4, which reduced ELSE IF chains and reorganized opcode handlers into grouped procedures.

Read: ~351, Work: 🔍 1,017

**src/sno_util.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32635** " 🔵 **READ_INPUT function implementation examined**

The READ_INPUT function implements dual-mode input handling for SNOBOL4 programs. In TTY mode (INP_TTY=1), it provides an interactive line editor reading from UART with backspace editing and Ctrl-D EOF signaling. In batch mode, it reads from a pre-loaded data file stored at INP_LOAD_ADDR, advancing through the buffer character by character until hitting a newline (0x0A) or null terminator (0x00). Both modes implement automatic uppercasing of ASCII lowercase letters by subtracting 32 from character codes 97-122, which explains why "Hello" prints as "HELLO" - this follows classic SNOBOL4 convention for case-insensitive keyword matching. Characters are stored in the string buffer (SB) starting at offset SBPOS, and the function returns the starting offset on success or -1 on end-of-file. The input.dat file contains standard Unix LF line endings verified by hexdump.

Read: ~414, Work: 🔍 2,328

**src/sno_exec.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32636** " 🔵 **OP_READ_INPUT opcode handler implementation reviewed**

The OP_READ_INPUT opcode handler in the executor implements INPUT operation with proper variable type tracking and EOF handling. When called, it invokes the READ_INPUT() utility function which returns either a string buffer offset on success or -1 on end-of-file. The success path (X_V >= 0) pushes the string offset onto the evaluation stack with type marker VTYP_STR and sets LAST_RESULT=1 to indicate success for subsequent conditional branching. The EOF path (X_V < 0) pushes a null string (offset 0) with VTYP_STR type and critically sets LAST_RESULT=0, which triggers the :F(label) failure branch mechanism used in the test program's `LINE = INPUT :F(DONE)` statement. Both paths maintain consistent type tracking by setting ETYP(ESP) and LAST_PUSH_TYP to VTYP_STR. This implementation appears correct and explains why the INPUT test is working properly despite issue #5 claiming type tracking or EOF handling was broken.

Read: ~399, Work: 🔍 2,004

**examples/dating.sno**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32637** 9:44 PM 🔵 **Additional INPUT-based programs tested successfully**

Testing additional SNOBOL4 programs that use INPUT operations confirms the functionality is working correctly across different use cases. The dating.sno program successfully reads data from its input file, processes strings through functions (HI function adds "** " prefix to names), and produces correct formatted output. The program executed 119,080 instructions and halted normally with no signs of the regression symptoms described in issue #5 - no garbage integers, no infinite looping, and proper string value handling throughout. This provides additional evidence that the INPUT regression either has been fixed or cannot be reproduced with the current codebase state after the sno_exec.plsw refactoring.

commit ba42f680bae9695486f59a0f8b98fbf199494bfb
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 10:46:53 2026 -0700

    m5: REMDR(A,B) numeric builtin + OP_MOD opcode
    
    Adds REMDR(A,B) as a two-argument numeric expression usable inside
    assignments (R = REMDR(7,3)). Recognized in the lexer's assign-
    expression path the same way SPAN/BREAK/ARRAY are, pushes its two
    operands into EP_TYP/EP_VAL, and uses S_OEOP=100 as an internal
    marker. Lowering then goes through the existing BINOP path, emitting
    LOAD, LOAD, OP_MOD.
    
    - Added OP_MOD=35 in am.msw (sw-cor24-plsw#30 landed, %DEFINE table
      now has headroom).
    - Executor pops two ints and computes A mod B by repeated subtraction;
      returns 0 if B <= 0.
    - examples/remdr.sno: R = REMDR(7,3); OUTPUT = R  prints 1.

commit bd6773efa01ee6a6fb962e1aa3357644b94da536
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Mon Apr 6 20:18:40 2026 -0700

    m4: user-defined functions (DEFINE, RETURN, FRETURN)
    
    Minimal end-to-end implementation:
    - DEFINE('FNNAME(PARAM)') registers a function in FN table
    - Function call expression FNNAME(arg) lowers to OP_CALL <fn_idx>
    - :(RETURN)/:(FRETURN) lower to OP_RET/OP_FRET
    - Call frame stack saves PC + param value across nested calls
    - Return value comes from the function-name variable (SNOBOL4 convention)
    
    Limitations: single argument per function, max 4 functions, call depth 16.
    Recursion supported but factorial-style demos need conditional builtins (step 2).
    
    examples/factorial.sno demos: DBL(X) returns 42 for input 21.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit ba42f680bae9695486f59a0f8b98fbf199494bfb
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 10:46:53 2026 -0700

    m5: REMDR(A,B) numeric builtin + OP_MOD opcode
    
    Adds REMDR(A,B) as a two-argument numeric expression usable inside
    assignments (R = REMDR(7,3)). Recognized in the lexer's assign-
    expression path the same way SPAN/BREAK/ARRAY are, pushes its two
    operands into EP_TYP/EP_VAL, and uses S_OEOP=100 as an internal
    marker. Lowering then goes through the existing BINOP path, emitting
    LOAD, LOAD, OP_MOD.
    
    - Added OP_MOD=35 in am.msw (sw-cor24-plsw#30 landed, %DEFINE table
      now has headroom).
    - Executor pops two ints and computes A mod B by repeated subtraction;
      returns 0 if B <= 0.
    - examples/remdr.sno: R = REMDR(7,3); OUTPUT = R  prints 1.

content.content:
Compare git history with saga history, report gaps

Walks first-parent git history on the current branch and matches commits to saga steps, either by the step's recorded commit hash (exact) or by timestamp window (heuristic fallback for legacy steps). Archived sagas' commits are treated as claimed and excluded from gap analysis.

Default output is a markdown report with four sections: matched, orphan commits (no matching step), orphan steps (no matching commit), and working-tree changes. With `--emit-commands`, prints a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` lines for orphan commits, pre-seeded from commit subjects. Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running the script.

Handles three cases: 1. Active saga with gaps: emits `add` lines for orphan commits. 2. No `.agentrail/` at all: emits `init --retroactive` followed by one `add` per historical commit, for bootstrapping an old repo. 3. Clean: reports no gaps.

Usage: agentrail audit [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --since <SINCE>
          Limit to commits after this git revision (e.g. "HEAD~50", "v1.0"). When omitted, scans all first-parent history on the current branch

      --emit-commands
          Emit a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` / `agentrail init` lines instead of a human-readable report. The script is a draft meant for human or agent review before execution — slugs and prompts are derived from commit subjects and usually need rewording

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')
---
Save a point-in-time copy of .agentrail/ into the git object store

Belt-and-suspenders recovery aid. Creates a proper git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>` containing a snapshot of `.agentrail/` (and `.agentrail-archive/` if present), then points a ref at it so the blobs are reachable and survive `git gc`. The user's real `.git/index` is never touched — snapshot operations run against a throwaway index via `GIT_INDEX_FILE`, so there are no staged-file side effects and no races with pre-commit hooks.

Intended use: run this manually before a risky agent operation, or after creating `.agentrail/` files you haven't yet committed, so that a stray `rm -rf` still has something to recover from. This is NOT a substitute for normal git tracking — it's a safety net for files that are not yet staged.

Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command: git restore --source=<ref> -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive

`agentrail snapshot --list` shows existing snapshot refs.

Usage: agentrail snapshot [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --list
          List existing snapshot refs instead of taking a new one

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.text:
Got it. Here's the plan — review and tell me if it's what you want.

## Available new commands

- **`agentrail snapshot`** — backs up `.agentrail/` to `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<ts>` (safety net before risky ops)
- **`agentrail snapshot --list`** — lists existing snapshots
- **`agentrail audit`** — diffs git history vs saga history, prints matched / orphan commits / orphan steps
- **`agentrail audit --emit-commands`** — emits a draft shell script of `agentrail init`/`agentrail add` lines pre-seeded from commit subjects
- **`agentrail add --commit <hash>`** — links a step to a specific commit (the retroactive bridge)
- **`agentrail init --retroactive`** — marks a saga as reconstructed; audit treats its commits as claimed

## What we have to recover

1. **`snobol4-demos`** saga (Apr 8, 3 steps: Sieve / N-Queens / ELIZA) — `saga.toml` + `plan.md` survive in `stash@{0}`; step dirs are gone.
2. **Unaccounted-for work after `snobol4-demos`** — ELIZA enhancements, demo batches, SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR, issue #4 fix, executor refactor (7+ commits). No saga ever existed for these on disk that I can find — either deleted without trace, or never created.

## Proposed plan

**Phase 0 — safety** (act now, no questions)
1. `agentrail snapshot` to checkpoint the current `.agentrail/` (m5 saga restored from `c840e5d`).
2. `agentrail snapshot --list` to confirm.

**Phase 1 — diagnose** (read-only)
3. `agentrail audit` against current branch — get a clean report of orphan commits since the m5 saga's commits.
4. `agentrail audit --emit-commands > /tmp/audit-draft.sh` — get the seeded shell script. Read but don't run.
5. Recover the `snobol4-demos` `saga.toml` + `plan.md` from `stash@{0}` to `/tmp/` for reference (don't write into `.agentrail/` yet).

**Phase 2 — discuss the reconstruction shape** (decision point — stop here for your input)

Before reconstructing, we need to agree on shape. Options:

- **(a) Two retroactive sagas:** archive current m5 saga → `init --retroactive snobol4-demos` with the recovered plan, `add --commit` each of sieve/nqueens/ELIZA, archive it → `init --retroactive` a third saga for the remaining orphans (post-ELIZA enhancements through executor refactor) with a fresh plan I draft from commit messages.
- **(b) One catch-all retroactive saga** "snobol4-post-fizzbuzz" covering everything from `e7257c3` onward, including the snobol4-demos work. Loses the original saga boundary but is simpler.
- **(c) Faithful reconstruction:** (a) plus also reconstruct intermediate sagas if I can find more evidence (e.g., other stashes, reflog blobs) of sagas that lived briefly after snobol4-demos.

I'd recommend **(a)** — it preserves the one boundary we have hard evidence for (snobol4-demos in stash) and consolidates the unknown post-demos work into one honest "reconstructed" saga rather than inventing boundaries.

**Phase 3 — execute** (only after you approve a shape)
6. Edit the draft from `audit --emit-commands` into the agreed-upon shape.
7. Archive the current m5 saga (it's already `completed`, this is housekeeping).
8. Run the reconstruction commands.
9. `agentrail audit` again — should report zero gaps.
10. `agentrail snapshot` final state.

## Questions before I start

1. Approve **Phase 0** + **Phase 1** to run now (all safe / read-only)?
2. Lean toward (a), (b), or (c) for Phase 2 — or want to wait until you see the audit output before deciding?

content.text:
The uncommitted files are a **SNOBOL4 runtime/engine module reorganization** in progress.

Currently tracked SNOBOL4 sources are split as:
- `sno_lex.plsw`, `sno_exec.plsw`, `sno_util.plsw`, `sno_main.plsw`

The three untracked files reorganize that into a different layout:

| File | Header says | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `snolib.plsw` (649 lines) | "SNOBOL4 runtime library — descriptor and heap header helper procedures, runtime core used by all SNOBOL4 programs" | **Runtime** — ships embedded with compiled programs |
| `sno_engine.plsw` (1654 lines) | "engine module: Lexer, parser, AM emitter, lowering, executor, pattern engine. Compiled as library — strip runtime before linking" | **Engine** — compiler/interpreter machinery |
| `snobol4.plsw` (1761 lines) | "SNOBOL4 interpreter for COR24" | **Main** — top-level driver |

This is a **runtime/engine separation** — splitting the runtime helpers (the part that needs to ship inside compiled SNOBOL4 programs) from the engine (the part that only the interpreter binary needs). It continues the modular-build theme from m4 (`linker-design.md`, `link24 modular build`) and prepares for compiled SNOBOL4 programs that bundle just `snolib`, not the whole engine.

## Name suggestions for the new active saga

In order of preference:

1. **`snobol4-runtime-split`** — most descriptive of what's actually happening (separating the runtime from the engine)
2. **`snolib-extraction`** — names the concrete artifact being created
3. **`m6-modular-runtime`** — fits the m1/m2/m3/m4/m5 milestone naming convention

I'd go with **`snobol4-runtime-split`** unless you want to keep the m-prefix convention (in which case `m6-runtime-split`).

Pick one (or propose your own) and I'll lock the full Phase 3 plan and run it.
Locked. Executing Phase 3 in three parts. Part 1 = Cluster A.
